parts.answer: The only correct answer is C (10) A is incorrect because this is the ratio by mass B is incorrect because in the mass ratio the unit of kg has been ignored and this is based on 10 mg of sulfur in 1 g of fuel D is incorrect because this is based on 10 g of sulfur in 1 kg of fuel (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is A (0.024 dm ) B is incorrect because the molar mass of sulfur has not been taken into account C is incorrect because the ma ss ratio has been ignored D is incorrect because the units of mg have been ignored (1) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is D (Cl + (g) → Cl 2+ (g) + e — ) A is incorrect because the equation re presents an electron is added to an ion rather than being removed B is incorrect because the equation repres ents electrons being added to an atom C is incorrect because one electron is being removed from each of two atoms (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is B (electrons are removed from molecules or atoms and positive ions are formed) A is incorrect because the sample has been vaporised previously C is incorrect because electrons are not a dded to atoms (in this mass spectrometer) D is incorrect because accel eration occurs in region S (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is A (ions with a greater mass have a smaller deflection) B is incorrect because ions with a gr eater mass have a smaller deflection C is incorrect because ions with a grea ter charge have a greater deflection D is incorrect because ions are no t speeded up in a magnetic field (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is B (0.029) A is incorrect because this is ju st the number of moles of sodium C is incorrect because this assumes th e number of moles of sodium is 0.1 D is incorrect because this is the concentration in g dm -3 (1) Mark Number 4(b) The only correct answer is A (120) B is incorrect because the mole ratio of 1:1 has been used C is incorrect because the mole ratio ha s been taken as 2:1 rather than 1:2 D is incorrect because the mass of sodium has been taken as the number of moles (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is A ( H + (aq) + OH − (aq) → H O(l) ) B is incorrect because the water has been ignored C is incorrect because the sulfuric acid has not been shown as ions D is incorrect because all the ions are shown but none are cancelled (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is A (29.1 %) B is incorrect because this is the percentage ratio of magnesiu m hydroxide to sodium sulfate C is incorrect because this is the percentage ratio of magnesiu m hydroxide to magnesium sulfate D is incorrect because this is the pe rcentage mole ratio of the product (1) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is C (N ― > O ― > F ― ) A is incorrect because the size of po sitive ions decreases across Period 3 B is incorrect because the size of posi tive ions increases down Group 1 D is incorrect because the size of ne gative ions increases down Group 6 (1) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is C (d) A is incorrect because the s block el ements don’t form stable 3+ ions B is incorrect because the elements which fo rm stable 3+ ions do not have 21 electrons D is incorrect because elements in th e f block have more than 21 electrons (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is D (ionic, covalent and dative covalent) A is incorrect because there are both dative covalent and cova lent bonds within the ions B is incorrect because there are dati ve covalent bonds within the ions C is incorrect because there are covalent bonds within the ions (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is C (3.01 x 10 ) A is incorrect because only on e ammonium, one iron and one sulfate ion have been included B is incorrect because the iron ions have not been included D is incorrect because the water mo lecules have been included (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is C (392.0) A is incorrect because the brackets have been ignored B is incorrect because the mass of water has been omitted D is incorrect because the iron has been doubled (1) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is B (Li< Be< B< C) A is incorrect because the melting temperatures of Group 7 elements increase going down the group C is incorrect because the melting temperatures of Group 1 elements decrease going down the group D is incorrect because silicon has the highest melting temperature in Period 3 (1) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is B (polar, non-polar) A is incorrect because the SF molecule is non-polar C is incorrect because the S-F bond is polar and the SF molecule is non-polar D is incorrect because the S-F bond is polar (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is B ( ) A is incorrect because the curly ar row indicates hete rolytic fission C is incorrect because both arrows are full arrows rather than half arrows D is incorrect because the arro ws are in the wrong direction (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is D (HCl) A is incorrect because ethane could be formed by the combination of two methyl radicals B is incorrect because chloromethane could be formed by th e combination of a methyl ra dical and a chlorine radical C is incorrect because dichloromethane coul d be formed from the combination of CH Cl radical and a chlorine radical (1) Question Number Mark

The only correct answer is A ( ) B is incorrect because it is Z-1-chlorobut-2-ene C is incorrect because it is Z-2-chlorobut-2-ene D is incorrect because it is E-1-chlorobut-2-ene (1) Cl Question Number Mark 11 (b) The only correct answer is D (11) A is incorrect because the C-H bonds a nd the sigma C-C have not been counted B is incorrect because some of the C-H bonds have not been counted C is incorrect because the C-C bond on th e alkene bond has not been counted (1) (Total for Section A = 20 marks) Section B Number Additional Guidance Mark
 2s  2s 2p 3s 3p 4s (1) (1) Ignore extra 1s for both Be and Ca Accept p x p y p z for p (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark

Answer that shows .....  2 single covalent bonds  6 non-bonding electrons shown on chlorine (1) (1) Example of correct dot-and-cross diagram: Allow non-bonding electrons unpaired Allow all dots or all crosses Allow shared electrons on the axis of the bond If Ca in place of Be Max 1 Ignore line showing covalent bond Ignore overlapping circles Ionic bond scores 0 overall Reject incorrect total number of electrons for M2 (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 a line/covalent bond is when electron(s) come from both/each atom(s)  arrow / dative covalent/ co-ordinate bond which is (a lone pair of) electrons donated from chlorine/ (only) one atom (1) (1) Ignore references to attraction between bonding pair and nucleus. Do not award any statement stating that the donation comes from Beryllium (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 bond angle in BeCl (g)/linear is 180 o  bond angle in BeCl (s)/polymeric is 109.5 o  electron pairs/bonds repel each other to minimise repulsion  4 electron pairs/bonds in solid or 2 bonding pairs/2 bonds (no lone pairs) in gas (1) (1) (1) (1) Ignore references to shape ALLOW any bond angle between 110 o and 95 o . [98 o actual value] Ignore references to shape Allow to a maximum separation Do not award repulsion between atoms/elements or between lone pairs Ignore references to lp-lp/lp-bp repulsion (4) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 correct charges shown and two chloride ions  electronic configuration of all ions (1) (1) Example of correct dot-and-cross diagram Accept Ca with 8 electrons Allow all dots or all crosses or any combination Ignore inner electron shells (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 Beryllium is smaller /has fewer electron shells/ has a higher charge density (than calcium) OR beryllium is more electronegative (than calcium)  Beryllium ion/ Be 2+ is more polarising (than calcium /ion Ca 2+ ) OR calcium loses (outer) electrons more easily (than beryllium) OR difference in electronegativity between calcium and chlorine is greater (than between beryllium and chlorine)  beryllium-chlorine bond has a higher degree of covalency (than calcium-chlorine bond) (1) (1) (1) Allow beryllium is more polarising than calcium Accept bonding in beryllium chloride is (more) covalent/ bonding in calcium chloride is (more) ionic. M3 is dependent on either M1 or M2 being scored Allow reverse arguments throughout (3) (Total for Question 12 = 15 marks) Number Additional Guidance Mark  expression for calculation of RAM of silicon  answer to two decimal places (1) (1) Example of calculation: (28x92.17) +(29x4.71)+(30x3.12) 100 =28.1095 =28.11 Allow units g mol -1 /a.m.u only Correct answer scores 2 Allow TE from incorrect expression if answer lies between 28 and 30. (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark Some silicon atoms (of mass 28) lose two electrons/have a charge of 2+ Allow (formation of ) Si 2+ Do not award “atoms of mass 14” (1) Number Additional Guidance Mark Si 14 14 Si 14 15 Si 14 16 (1) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 all atoms are joined together by covalent bonds/giant covalent(structure)  these are strong / take a lot of energy to break (so melting temperature is very high)  no electrons are free (to move)/ mobile/delocalised/ (so charge cannot be carried) (1) (1) (1) Allow correct description of covalent bond Do not award intermolecular forces/ ionic bonds/ double covalent bonds. Ignore stoi chiometry even if incorrect. Ignore high boiling temperature Ignore no free moving ions (3) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 calculation of number of moles  calculation of whole number mole ratio  empirical formula (1) (1) (1) Example of calculation 12 8.43 14.47 40.1 28.1 16 OR 0.299 : 0.30 : 0.904 1 : 1 : 3 CaSiO Correct formula with no working scores 1 mark only TE on use of atomic numbers Incorrect symbol(s) in formula loses M3 (3) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 calculation of moles carbon dioxide  conversion of temperature to K  rearrangement of expression  evaluation of answer in cm (1) (1) (1) (1) Example of calculation: 3÷44 = 0.06818 273 + 5 = 278 V=0.06818 x 8.31 x 278 1.3 x 10 (=1.2116 x 10 − m ) =1211.6/1212/1210/1200 (cm ) TE throughout but do not award M4 for negative volume Ignore SF except 1 SF Correct answer with no working scores 4 marks (4) (Total for Question 13 = 14 marks) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 there is a “jump”/much bigger increase between the third and fourth ionisation energies  (First) 3 electrons in outer shell (1) (1) Accept fourth electron is removed from a different (quantum) shell (which is closer to the nucleus) (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark  the number of protons increases /nuclear charge increases  the additional electrons are in the same shell/same energy l evel/ little additional shielding from electrons (1) (1) Allow “same shielding” Ignore references to atomic radius even if incorrect Ignore electron repulsion (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark  the first electron is removed from a (3)p orbital/subshell in aluminium/ (3)s orbital/subshell from magnesium  p electron(s) shielded by the s electrons (so requires less energy)/ p energy level is higher than s energy level (1) (1) Do not award 2p/2s (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to  delocalised /free (to move) electrons /sea of electrons  attracted to (a lattice of) metal/aluminium/positive ions/cations (1) (1) Allow charges up to 3+ Correct fully labelled diagram scores 2 marks Ignore attraction of electrons to nuclei (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to two of the following:  (delocalised) electrons can move/carry charge  aluminium has low density/light (weight)  aluminium is ductile/can be drawn into wires  aluminium forms an inert/oxide layer (1) (1) (1) (1) Allow good conductor of electricity Ignore malleable, strong, he at resistant, high melting temperature. (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark Al + H O  Al O + 3H Allow multiples Ignore state symbols even if incorrect (1) Number Additional Guidance Mark An answer that makes reference to two of the following:  reduces greenhouse gas emissions / carbon dioxide and which affects climate change / global warming  the production of hydrogen is portable  fossil fuels are non-renewable (unlike water)  aluminium can be recycled (1) (1) (1) (1) Allow reduces

An answer that makes reference to:  the mixture is boiled/ vapourised/ (fractionally) distilled  distillate condensing at a suitable temperature (range) is collected (1) (1) Do not award cracking/reforming Allow correct references to co llecting fractions at different heights/temperatures (in the fractionating column) Allow simple descriptions of fractional distillation e.g. separated by boiling point/ temperature Do not award references to molecular mass or to melting temperature. (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark (C H → 2C H +) C H (1) Number Additional Guidance Mark  cis and trans skeletal formulae  2-methylpropene (1) (1) and Allow 1 mark for three correct non-skeletal formulae Ignore bond lengths and angles (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark Product Name Structural formula 1,2- dichlorobutane CH CH CHC l CH C l butane CH CH CH CH butan(e)-1,2- diol CH CH CHOHCH OH butan(e)-2-ol CH CH CHOHCH (4) 8 correct points scores 4 marks 6 or 7 correct points scores 3 marks 4 or 5 correct points scores 2 marks 2 or 3 correct points scores 1 mark Allow displayed / skeletal formulae If two types of formula are shown both must be correct to score the point. Ignore punctuation errors , misplaced numbers e.g. 1,2-butan diol and connectivity of pendant -OH group. Penalise omission of “an” in 3 and 4 once only Penalise wrong number of carbon atoms once only Award 1 point for butan-1-ol and correct formula in Product 4 (4) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 correct repeat unit  two repeat units and continuation bonds ALLOW C H , CH CH as pendant group (1) (1) Ignore brackets and n Allow ethyl groups on C2 and C3 or C1 and C4 M2 depends on M1 or near miss e.g. attachment of pendant group or use of al ternative 4-carbon monomer alkene producing a valid polymer i.e. poly(but-2-ene)/poly(methylpropene). Do not award a dimer (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark

 energy is produced /can be used to generate electricity or heat locally Or prevents polymers going to landfill/storage/taking up space  toxic/harmful/corrosive gases/substances may be produced Or particulates/soot may be produced (1) (1) Allow production of hydrogen Ignore non-biodegradable, non-renewable, reduces polymer waste/pollution a named gas e.g. sulfur dioxide, hydrogen cyanide, dioxin without qualification is insufficient Do not award references to climate change (2) Number Additional Guidance Mark  volume CO produced  volume of oxygen used  total volume of gas remaining Alternative method  moles CO produced  moles oxygen remaining  total volume remaining (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) Example of calculation: 4 x 35 = 140 (cm ) 6.5 x 35 = 227.5/228 (cm ) 300-227.5 + 140 (cm ) =212.5/213 (cm ) Alternative method 35 / 24000 = 1.4583 x 10 -3 / 1.458 x 10 -3 /1.46 x 10 -3 1.4583 x 10 -3 x 4 = 5.8333 x 10 -3 / 5.83 x 10 -3 or 5.84 x 10 -3 if rounded previously 300/24000 = 1.25 x 10 -2 (1.46 x 10 -3 ) x 6.5 = 9.49 x 10 -3 moles (1.25 x 10 -2 ) – (9.49 x 10 -3 ) = 3.01 x 10 -3 (mol) [(5.83 x 10 -3 ) + (3.01 x 10 -3 )] x 24000 = 212.16/212 (cm ) Allow TE throughout. Correct answer with no working scores 3 marks IGNORE SF except 1 SF (3) Number Additional Guidance Mark  carbon monoxide may be produced (incomplete combustion may occur)  which is toxic/combines(irreversibly) with haemoglobin/ (1) (1) Do not award particulates M2 depends on clear link to gas produced by burning butane Ignore references to flammability of butane/global warming/greenhouse gases If no other mark awarded allow 1 mark for carbon dioxide causes suffocation. (2) (Total for Question 15 = 18 marks) (Total for Section B = 60 marks) (Total for Paper = 80 marks) Pearson Education Limited.
